Press ESC or click outside to close
Workflow · 01 · B · Phase 01 Analysis · Run by Analyst (Mary)
Research Trio
Three orthogonal investigation tracks — Market / Domain / Technical — run independently or combined; output research-findings.md with full citations APA/Chicago format feed directly into Phase 2.
what

Research workflow is 3 orthogonal tracks run independently or combine depending on need. Each track has the same 6-step pattern but different internal content — Mary (Analyst) facilitate evidence gathering, triangulate sources, synthesize insights, deliver evidence-based recommendations.

3 research dimensions that the user can explicitly pick 1, 2, or all 3:

Dimension Covers When needed
MarketTAM/SAM/SOM, competitors mapping, trends, user sentiment, pricing benchmarksB2B/B2C concept has competitors; validate market size and positioning before commitment
DomainVocabulary, expert mental models, regulations (HIPAA/GDPR/PCI), industry constraintsIndustry-specific domain (healthcare/finance/legal); must native terminology + compliance map
TechnicalFeasibility, stack options, implementation paths, vendor analysis, library/framework comparisonNovel tech choice; needs feasibility validation before PRD commit specific approach

6-step pattern shared by each dimension:

01
Define the research question — 5 Whys from surface question to root; turn it into 5-8 sub-questions answerable with evidence
02
Collect evidence — 4 source types (web / official docs / expert content / interview notes), each finding gets a quality grade (primary/secondary/tertiary)
03
Cross-reference + find signals — build finding matrix, scan 3 signal types: patterns (≥3 sources), contradictions (conflicts), gaps (empty cells)
04
Synthesize insights — distill into 3-5 actionable insights, quantify when data exists, attach a confidence level (high/medium/low)
05
Evidence-based recommendations — Given/Recommend/Because/Owner/Metric/Risk format, 3-7 ranked recs
06
Save report — generate research-findings.md with full citations APA/Chicago + invoke bmad-help

Output artifact: research-findings.md saved in _bmad-output/planning-artifacts/ containing: executive summary, methodology section, sub-questions with evidence per question, insights (confidence-rated), ranked recommendations, full bibliography.

why

Building on assumptions = building things nobody needs. Research workflow prevents scenarios where the team commits resources to PRD/architecture and only later discovers an assumption was wrong. Cost of late discovery = 10-100x cost of upfront research.

Each dimension catches a different blind spot that running only one dimension cannot cover:

Dimension skip Blind spot resulting Typical failure mode
Skip Market researchStrong competitors, market saturation, and user willingness-to-pay are unknownShip product with pricing user not accept; discover competitor had the same feature 2 years before
Skip Domain researchWrong vocabulary, missed regulation, poor understanding of expert users' mental modelsHealthcare app uses marketer language that doctors reject; HIPAA violation at launch
Skip Technical researchWrong feasibility estimate, vendor lock-in that will not scale, missed alternative stacksPhase 4 discover chosen DB not support compliance requirement; rewrite epic 1

Source triangulation is core of research integrity. A fact appearing in 3 independent sources = evidence; a fact in 1 source = assumption. Quality grading helps weighted synthesis:

Grade Examples Confidence weight
PrimaryRegulator docs, SEC filings, official company statements, direct interview notesHigh — first-hand or authoritative; often dispositive
SecondaryReuters/Bloomberg journalism, peer-reviewed research, industry reports from Gartner/ForresterMedium — reputable but second-hand; cross-check when conflict
TertiaryWikipedia, aggregator sites, blog opinions, forum discussionsLow — useful for hypotheses formation, never sole source

Pattern recognition is not the only signal. Mary tracks 3 signal types, each with value:

(1) Patterns ≥3 sources confirming = high-confidence finding; (2) Contradictions between sources = contested ground, a signal worth digging into (regulatory uncertainty, market disagreement); (3) Gaps = unknown unknowns, predictive about risks in Phase 3-4.

Research without recommendations = trivia. PM/founder needs a decision, not a fact list. Format Given [evidence] → Recommend [action] → Because [reasoning] forces Mary to articulate a logic chain traceable to sources — defensible in stakeholder meetings.

Citations required with URL + date + quote — prevents AI from hallucinating stats. 6 months after, readers can trace each claim to its source. Reproducibility = trust = defendability when PRD before Board.

how

Activation: Mary supports 3 separate commands — user has specific invoke 1 or many:

Command Short-code Workflow file
bmad-market-researchMR_bmad/bmm/workflows/1-analysis-workflows/market-research/
bmad-domain-researchDR_bmad/bmm/workflows/1-analysis-workflows/domain-research/
bmad-technical-researchTR_bmad/bmm/workflows/1-analysis-workflows/technical-research/

Mary recommend dimension(s) based on project context. Vd: B2B SaaS startup typically must Market + Domain; novel tech project must Technical + Domain; established domain has an existing brief usually only needs Market.

5 Whys protocol in step 01 — example example concrete to illustrate depth:

Level Question evolution
Q0"Learn about AI for documents" (surface, vague)
Q1Why? "To know whether there is a market for AI document Q&A"
Q2Why? "Because the team wants to build a product in this space"
Q3Why? "Because has hypothesis enterprise customers will pay for privacy-first solution"
Q4Why? "Because on-device LLM technology has just become mature enough"
Q5Root: "Are there competitors using on-device LLMs for document Q&A, and what is their pricing range?"

The root question is now specific + answerable with evidence — not an opinion question. Mary then splits it into 5-8 sub-questions, each narrow enough for one search session to answer.

Multi-query strategy in step 02 — Mary rephrase each sub-question 2-3 different ways to cover keyword space:

Approach Examples for query "on-device LLM document apps"
Literal search"on-device LLM document Q&A apps"
Synonym shift"local AI document chatbot" · "private LLM document search"
Use-case angle"enterprise private document AI" · "GDPR compliant document AI"
Technical angle"llama.cpp document RAG" · "Ollama document Q&A"
Competitor angle"document AI alternatives to OpenAI"

Finding matrix structure in step 03:

Source A (primary) Source B (secondary) Source C (secondary) Source D (tertiary)
Sub-Q1Direct stat ✓Confirming quote ✓—Conflicting opinion ⚠
Sub-Q2—Detailed analysis ✓——
Sub-Q3————

The matrix exposes signals visually: Sub-Q1 has pattern (2 sources confirming) + contradiction (Source D); Sub-Q2 weak (only 1 secondary source); Sub-Q3 is gap (needs more research or accept-risk).

Insight format in step 04 — each insight 1 line claim + evidence pointer + confidence:

📊 Insight: Market consolidating around top 3 vendors who control ~70% volume (Sources: 1, 3, 5). Confidence: High (3 primary sources, recent <6 months).

Recommendation format in step 05 — Given/Recommend/Because/Owner/Metric/Risk:

Given: 70% market concentration among top 3 vendors (Insight 1)
Recommend: Pursue niche segment X (vertical industry vertical Y) instead of horizontal play
Because: Direct competition with best-fit incumbents requires capital we don't have; niche allows differentiation
Owner: PM
Success metric: 100 paying customers in vertical Y within 6 months
Risk if skipped: Direct compete → burn rate exceeds runway by month 9

Citation format in step 06 — APA or Chicago style:

Style Format
APAAuthor, A. (Year). Title. Publisher. URL. Retrieved YYYY-MM-DD.
ChicagoAuthor Name, "Title," Publisher, Date, URL. (With footnotes if extended discussion)

Handoff lifecycle at final step 06: AI invokes bmad-help with arg="research". Help skill evaluate strength of findings:

01·B done
→
01·C Product Brief
→
02·A PRD

Path 1: insights solid → document concept with brief → PRD

01·B done
→
01·D PR/FAQ
→
02·A PRD

Path 2: insights needs stress-testing → PR/FAQ Amazon Working Backwards before PRD

01·B done
→
01·B (other dimensions)

Path 3: insights weak → add another research dimension (already Market? add Domain or Technical)

Press ESC or click outside to close
Workflow · 01 · C · Phase 01 Analysis · Run by Analyst (Mary)
Product Brief
1-2 page executive summary — vision/mission + personas + problem statement + value prop + metrics + constraints; 'gentler path' into PRD so with PR/FAQ rigorous stress-test.
what

Product Brief is a 1-2 page executive summary that compresses the full planning context into a document stakeholders can read in 5 minutes to decide go/no-go. Mary co-drafts with the user through probing questions — does not mechanically fill a template.

Brief vs PRD — clear distinction:

Aspect Product Brief (01·C) PRD (02·A)
Length1-2 pagesMultiple pages (3-13 sections depending on Level)
PurposeArticulate concept + buy-inContract spec for engineering
AudienceFounders + early team + stakeholdersArchitect + Dev + QA + Designer
When writtenConcept is already clear and needs documentationAfter brief, before architecture
Detail levelStrategic — what + who + whyTactical — FRs/NFRs/AC/decisions

8 steps weeks self:

01
Init + scope — load prior artifacts (brainstorm/research/PRFAQ), ask 3 question scope (audience/decision/detail level), propose outline
02
Vision + Mission — co-draft 5-10 years vision + 1-question mission by Geoffrey Moore template
03
Target users + personas — 1-3 personas with JTBD format, demographics, JTBD, primary pain, current alternative
04
Problem statement — what/who/why important/why now, false-able, not solution-in-the-problem
05
Value proposition — Value Proposition Canvas (pain relievers + gain creators), differentiation matrix
06
Success metrics — North Star + 3-5 HEART framework KPIs, baseline + target + measurement source
07
Constraints — 5 types (budget/timeline/team/technical/regulatory) × 3 tiers (MUST/SHOULD/MAY)
08
Save + handoff — write exec summary last (placed first per Pyramid Principle), save product-brief.md, invoke bmad-help

Output structure:

Section Content
Executive summary1 paragraph — written last, placed first per Pyramid Principle (conclusion-first)
Vision + Mission5-10 year vision + 1-question mission via Geoffrey Moore template
Target users1-3 personas — demographics + JTBD + primary pain + current alternative + success criteria
Problem statementWhat + Who + Why important + Why now (4 components, false-able)
Value propositionPain relievers + gain creators + differentiation vs 3 alternatives (competitors + workaround + do-nothing)
Success metricsNorth Star + HEART KPIs with baseline/target/source
Constraints5 types × 3 tiers — MUST/SHOULD/MAY categorized
Next stepsRecommended Phase 2 workflow (PRD or PR/FAQ first)
why

Product Brief is a "gentler path" into PRD — suitable when the concept is already clear and team must articulate to get buy-in before committing PRD effort. Brief lighter weight, forces tight 1-2 page writing, forces clear trade-offs.

Brief vs PR/FAQ — 2 paths into PRD:

Path When to use Rationale
Brief (01·C)Concept is clear, the team agrees on direction, and documentation is needed to align stakeholdersGentler — articulate without challenge. Fits when brainstorming + research are already solid
PR/FAQ (01·D)Concept is uncertain, needs stress-testing before commitment, and the team has hypotheses that are not yet solidRigorous — adversarial questioning. Fits when uncertainty high, risk acceptance is needed before build

The two paths are not mutually exclusive — the user can run both (brief to document, PR/FAQ to stress-test) or just one. PRD workflow extracts the brief automatically during the Discovery phase, reducing re-explanation.

The 1-2 page format forces tight writing. Quality over quantity. A section that is too long = signal not yet clearly understood. An empty section = signal missing evidence. Compression forces the user to articulate value instead of hiding it in verbosity.

Each section addresses 1 critical question that stakeholder will ask:

Section Question it answers
Vision + Mission"Long-term direction of product is what?" + "Mission specific specific what-for-whom-how?"
Personas"Who exactly is the primary user? Are they real, or are they an 'average user' fiction?"
Problem statement"What problem is being solved? For whom? Why is it important? Why now?"
Value proposition"Value unique vs alternatives? Why users switch?"
Success metrics"How will success be measured? What is the North Star metric?"
Constraints"Boundaries of solution space? MUST/SHOULD/MAY?"

"Average user" is fiction — products that try to serve everyone usually serve no one. 1-3 personas enough cover most use cases that still force choices. Real-world examples anchor personas in reality.

Solution-in-the-problem is antipattern. "We need a chatbot" = solution disguised. "Users spend 4hr/week answering same support questions, frustrating both customers and team" = problem. Statement must be falsifiable — can be disproven with evidence.

Constraints define solution space. Not listing them = assuming they do not exist = surprises during the sprint. Conflict between 2 MUST constraints must be resolved before commitment — not during development. Constraint missed at brief level → architecture wrong → 10x cost to fix later.

Vanity metrics killer. Page views, downloads, signups not correlate with business value. Brief specify outcome metrics (retention, engagement, task completion) — not output metrics (features shipped). HEART framework forces 5 dimensions: Happiness / Engagement / Adoption / Retention / Task success.

how

Activation: Mary is invoked through /bmad-agent-bmm-analyst then PB. Or use the "Hey Mary, write product brief" shortcut.

Configuration Detail
Commandbmad-product-brief · short-code PB
Workflow path_bmad/bmm/workflows/1-analysis-workflows/product-brief/
Step location8 micro-files: step-01.md ... step-08.md
Output path_bmad-output/planning-artifacts/product-brief.md

Prior artifact extraction in step 01 — Mary scan workspace for artifacts from Phase 1:

Artifact present What Mary extract
brainstorming-report.mdTop picks → candidate ideas; clusters → potential personas; rationale → vision direction
research-findings.mdMarket insights → problem statement evidence; competitors → differentiation matrix; constraints → regulatory/technical sections
prfaq-{project}.mdPress release → vision condensed; customer quotes → personas validation; External FAQ → value prop articulated; CX narrative → user journeys

Extract (not re-derive) saves time and keep consistency with earlier decisions. Mary not re-ask questions already answered in prior artifacts — bypass redundancy.

Vision crafting protocol in step 02 — Mary use 2 frameworks in parallel:

Framework Template Test pass
Simon Sinek "Start with Why"Vision = belief about a future state, 5-10 years timelineOpposite test: opposite must be plausible. If the opposite does not make sense → vision is too bland
Geoffrey Moore positioning"For [user], who [pain], our [product] is [category] that [unique value]. Unlike [alternative], we [differentiator]."Compress down to 1-2 questions. Read-aloud test — generic? redo

Persona template in step 03 — JTBD format of Clayton Christensen:

Sarah, 32, Operations Manager at mid-size healthcare clinic, Boston, MA, tech-savvy intermediate
JTBD: "When patients call asking about insurance coverage, I want to give them accurate answer immediately, so I can avoid 3-day waiting period and reduce frustration."
Primary pain: Spending 4hr/day looking up insurance details in 6 different systems; high error rate damages patient trust
Current alternative: Manually checking each insurance portal + calling insurance reps when ambiguous (slow, error-prone)
Success criteria: 90% queries answered < 2 minutes, < 5% error rate, patient NPS +15

Validate persona: Mary asks "Do you know a real person who matches this persona?". If not → persona may be fiction, redo with real example.

Anti-persona mandatory — who we explicitly DON'T serve. Vd: "Solo practitioners with < 100 patients/month — too small for our pricing tier, will churn quickly". Anti-persona prevents product scope creep.

Problem statement format in step 04 — 4-component PAIN framework:

Component Format Validation test
WhatSpecific, not a disguised solutionIf it contains a product-like noun ("chatbot", "dashboard") → solution leak, redo
WhoLink to specific persona(s)Generic "users" → vague, not testable
Why importantFrequency × Severity × Visibility quantified"High" / "Critical" without metric → vanity claim
Why nowTiming trigger (regulation/tech/market shift)"Because we want to" → not a why-now

Value Proposition Canvas in step 05 — Strategyzer methodology:

A
Customer pains — linked to problem statement step 04
B
Customer gains — positive outcomes user desires (time saved, status gained, money earned)
C
Pain relievers — how product addresses each pain (mapped 1-1)
D
Gain creators — how product creates each gain (mapped 1-1)

Differentiation matrix — rows = features, cols = us + top 3 competitors + workaround + do-nothing. Mary flag features that all competitors has = table stakes (not differentiator). 1-2 "only we have because [moat]" statements articulated.

HEART framework for success metrics in step 06:

Letter Dimension Example metric
HHappinessNPS, satisfaction score, CSAT
EEngagementDAU/MAU, session frequency, feature adoption
AAdoptionSignup conversion, time-to-first-value, activation rate
RRetentionD7, D30, cohort retention curves, monthly churn
TTask successCompletion rate, error rate, time per task

North Star Metric framework of Sean Ellis — 1 metric that if improved, the entire business improves. It must be a leading indicator of revenue.

Constraint tier categorization in step 07:

Tier Definition Example (healthcare app)
MUSTDeal-breaker if violatedHIPAA compliant, on-device for sensitive data, < $500K budget
SHOULDPreferred but flexibleMobile-first UX, integration with Epic EHR, launch by Q3
MAYNice-to-haveMulti-language support, custom branding for clinics, AI insights

Handoff at final step 08:

01·C done
→
02·A PRD

Path 1: concept already clear, go directly to PRD — Mary's brief feeds John automatically

01·C done
→
01·D PR/FAQ
→
02·A PRD

Path 2: concept needs stress-testing → PR/FAQ then PRD

Press ESC or click outside to close
Workflow · 01 · D · Phase 01 Analysis · Run by Analyst (Mary) (v6.7 ownership)
PRFAQ Challenge
Amazon's Working Backwards methodology — write press release as if it is launch day before one line of code exists, then External FAQ + Internal FAQ with a pre-mortem protocol to stress-test the concept.
what

PR/FAQ is Amazon's Working Backwards methodology invented by Jeff Bezos. Reverse process: write press release as if it is launch day before one line of code exists, then answer hard questions from skeptical customers + adversarial team/board. A concept that cannot pass the PR/FAQ stress-test = not ready to build.

Run by Analyst (Mary) — v6.7 BMM Core assign full ownership of PRFAQ Challenge for Mary with trigger WB (Working Backwards). John (PM) is downstream consumer of PRFAQ output (artifact feeds the PRD workflow), but does not own this workflow anymore.

5 stages weeks self:

01
Press Release — write the PR as if it is launch day, in AP/Reuters style: headline + subhead + lead + body + customer quotes + leadership quote + CTA. ~1 page
02
External FAQ — 8-12 Q/A from skeptical customer angle: pricing/comparison/risks/migration/support. Hardest questions at the top
03
Internal FAQ — pre-mortem protocol: top risks, cost breakdown, timeline assumptions, "what if we're wrong about X". Categorize risks by Market/Tech/Team/Regulatory/Financial
04
Customer Experience narrative — tell the end-to-end journey: awareness → consideration → onboarding → first use → habitual use. Each stage has a target emotion + moments of truth
05
Stress-test + finalize — adversarial review from 5 angles (investor/customer/engineer/competitor/regulator), grade answers strong/weak/missing, patch gaps

Output structure of prfaq-{project}.md:

Section Length Purpose
Press Release~1 pageArticulate value in user-facing language as if launched today
External FAQ8-12 Q/AAddress customer skepticism + edge questions
Internal FAQ8-12 Q/APre-mortem risks + cost breakdown + assumption testing
CX Narrative1-2 pagesEnd-to-end user journey with an emotion curve + moments of truth
Stress-test results1 pageAdversarial review findings + gap documentation

Press Release components:

Component Length Purpose
Headline8-12 wordsAttention-grabbing, articulate core value
Subhead1 questionExpand on headline, add specificity
Lead paragraph2-3 questionWho/what/when/where/why (inverted pyramid)
Body2-3 paragraphsDetail product + key features + value
Customer quotes2-3 quotesConcrete outcomes (vd: "saved me 4hr/week") — not vague "I love it"
Leadership quote1 quoteFrom CEO/founder articulating theory of change
CTA1 lineWhere to learn more / sign up
why

Working Backwards methodology was invented by Bezos because of the observation: most products fail not because of bad engineering, but because they are not different enough to user switch. If you cannot write a compelling PR, the product is not ready to build.

PR forces articulation of the value proposition in user-facing language, is not engineer-facing. "GraphQL backend with React Native frontend" = engineer talk; "Get insurance answers in seconds, not days" = user value.

Boredom test — read PR aloud. If it sounds boring, the product is not exciting enough. A boring PR predicts a boring product launch — not one users will care about.

5 hardest questions real — pre-mortem mindset:

Angle Hardest question
Investor"What's TAM? Unit economics? Moat that prevents copying?"
Customer"Why would I switch from [current solution]? What's the risk if I commit?"
Engineer"Is this technically feasible at scale? What breaks at 100x users?"
Competitor"How quickly can we copy this? What's their unfair advantage?"
Regulator"What compliance does this need? Privacy/security implications?"

Pre-mortem protocol by Daniel Kahneman — assume product fails within 12 months, tell the story of WHY. Research shows pre-mortem improves forecast accuracy by 30% compared with no pre-mortem. Surface implicit assumptions before commitment — after commit cost 10-100x to fix.

FAQs expose uncomfortable questions teams often avoid. Pricing comparison, data privacy, vendor lock-in, migration cost — all questions hide behind launch hype. Being able to answer before launch = ready to defend the product; unable to answer = not solid yet, signal to rework.

Hardest questions on top — not bury at the bottom of FAQ. If the team is not sure of the answer = signal must resolve before commit. Skeptical framing catches issues that launch hype miss.

Customer Experience narrative vs static features list — clear distinction:

Features list (engineering view) CX narrative (user view)
"Has search, filters, export, audit log""User opens app, sees 3 patient questions waiting; types one, gets instant answer with source; feels confident, responds in seconds"
What it does technicallyWhat user feels at each moment
Hides flow problemsReveals transitions where dropoff happens
No moments of truthHighlights critical decision points

Most UX problems live at transitions between stages, not in the stages themselves. Static screens hide flow. Narrative exposes gaps that a spec sheet does not reveal.

Peak-end rule (Kahneman): users remember peak emotion + end emotion of experience. Design deliberate peak (e.g. instant aha moment) + strong end (e.g. clean completion confirmation). Skip narrative = miss peak-end opportunity.

Stress-test is moment of truth of PR/FAQ workflow. If the PR/FAQ withstands 10 adversarial questions from 5 angles, the concept already has resilience. If it cannot get through, return to brainstorming or missing research dimensions. Stress-testing replaces a post-launch market reality check with cheap, on-paper validation before an expensive build.

Gap documentation mandatory. When adversarial review finds a question that cannot be answered, document it explicitly in the research backlog — do not pretend it is solid. The Phase 2 PRD then knows what needs further investigation.

how

Activation: Analyst (Mary) own workflow. Invoke through /bmad-agent-bmm-analyst then WB (Working Backwards). Or named-agent shortcut: "Hey Mary, run PRFAQ challenge".

Configuration Detail
Primary agentMary (📊 Business Analyst) — _bmad/bmm/agents/analyst.md
Downstream consumerJohn (📋 PM) — PRFAQ output feeds the PRD workflow in Phase 2
Commandbmad-create-prfaq · short-code WB (Working Backwards)
Workflow path_bmad/bmm/workflows/1-analysis-workflows/prfaq/
Stages location5 stage files: stage-01.md ... stage-05.md
Output path_bmad-output/planning-artifacts/prfaq-{project}.md

Press Release writing protocol (stage 1) — AP/Reuters journalism style:

HEADLINE: ClinicAI Now Answers Insurance Questions in 30 Seconds, Free Healthcare Workers from Daily 4-Hour Lookups

SUBHEAD: First HIPAA-compliant AI assistant trained on every major US insurance policy launches today, available to all medical practices.

LEAD: Boston, March 2026 — ClinicAI today announced general availability of its AI assistant that answers any insurance coverage question in under 30 seconds, eliminating the 3-day waiting period that has frustrated patients and medical practices for decades...

CUSTOMER QUOTE: "Before ClinicAI, I spent 4 hours daily looking up insurance details. Now those 4 hours go back to patients. Our NPS jumped 30 points," — John Chen, Operations Manager at Westside Medical Group

Customer quote test — concrete > vague:

Bad (vague) Good (concrete outcome)
"I love this product""Saved me 4 hours per week"
"It's really helpful""Reduced patient wait time from 3 days to 30 seconds"
"Game changer""Cut our insurance-related errors by 78%"

External FAQ topics mandatory coverage (stage 2):

Topic Hardest question patterns
Pricing"Why is it this expensive?" · "Is there a free tier?" · "What if I only need 5% of features?"
Comparison"How is this different from [competitor X]?" · "Why not use ChatGPT?"
Risk"What about data privacy?" · "Vendor lock-in concerns?" · "What if you go out of business?"
Migration"How do I migrate from current solution?" · "Learning curve?"
Support"What if it breaks?" · "Refund policy?" · "SLA guarantees?"

FAQ answer protocol: 2-4 line/answer, direct + concrete + escape hatch if applicable. Avoid marketing fluff — concrete numbers/comparisons.

Pre-mortem protocol in stage 3 — Internal FAQ:

1
Assume product fails in 12 months — set the scene in the future
2
Tell the story of WHY — detailed narrative about failure (not just "did not work")
3
Identify top 5 failure reasons — extract from narrative
4
Convert into risk register — categorize by 5 dimensions, score probability × impact

Risk categorization:

Category Sub-types Example failure mode
MarketDemand, timing, competition"Apple launches similar feature in 6 months, kills our differentiator"
TechnicalFeasibility, scalability, vendor"On-device LLM accuracy degrades on rare insurance types, user trust collapses"
TeamSkills, capacity, retention"Lead ML engineer leaves Q2, team can't ship critical model improvement"
RegulatoryCompliance, data, jurisdiction"HIPAA audit fails, can't ship to medical practices, runway burns"
FinancialCAC, LTV, runway"CAC higher than LTV, unit economics fail, can't raise next round"

Each risk in the register: probability (H/M/L) × impact (H/M/L) + early warning signal + mitigation owner.

CX narrative protocol in stage 4 — 5 stages with an emotion curve:

Stage Target emotion Moment of truth
AwarenessCuriousFirst impression — does it solve my problem?
ConsiderationCautiously optimisticPricing page — perceived value vs cost
OnboardingConfidentFirst 5 minutes — overwhelm or empowerment?
First useDelighted (aha moment)Value realization — does it actually work?
Habitual useReliant, advocateDaily/weekly engagement loop, NPS moment

Service Blueprint methodology — above-the-line (user-visible) + below-the-line (backend support) — reveal what user sees + what makes it work.

Adversarial stress-test in stage 5 — John cycle through 5 personas, each persona 2-3 hard questions:

Grade Criteria Action
StrongEvidence-based, concrete, defensibleKeep as-is
WeakHas answer but hand-wavy or one-sourcePatch/strengthen section related
MissingNo good answer availableFlag as gap, document for research backlog

Iterate until all answers are ≥ "strong" or gaps are explicitly documented. This adversarial review pattern is reused in bmad-code-review with fresh review context — same philosophy of "must find issues, not rubber-stamp".

Handoff:

01·D done
→
02·A PRD

Path 1: PRFAQ pass → PRD direct, PRFAQ feed PRD workflow automatically

01·D done
→
01·B Research
→
02·A PRD

Path 2: PRFAQ surface knowledge gaps → research more before PRD

Press ESC or click outside to close
Workflow · 02 · A · Phase 02 Planning · Run by PM (John)
PRD
Single intent-detecting bmad-prd skill (create / update / validate) that produces the Product Requirements Document. REQUIRED in Phase 2 — does not have PRD then not into Phase 3. Scale-adaptive depth based Level 0-4 and domain complexity. Adds a .memlog.md audit trail, an addendum.md overflow doc, an Essential Spine + Adapt-In Menu section model, and a Reviewer Gate with HTML report. Supersedes the deprecated create/edit/validate PRD shims.
what

PRD workflow is required gate between Phase 1 (Analysis) and Phase 3 (Solutioning). John (PM) co-author with user — does not fill the template mechanically; instead, probes deeper with "why?" relentlessly to surface implicit user needs.

Tri-modal architecture — 3 modes operate on same PRD:

Mode Short-code When to use
Create / Validate / Updatebmad-prdSingle intent-detecting skill: build PRD from scratch, quality-check an existing PRD, or apply surgical append-only updates — all through one entry point with a .memlog.md audit trail

13 steps in Create mode (Levels 3-4) — scale-adaptive for Level 0-1:

01
Init + scope — load Phase 1 artifacts, define in-scope / out-of-scope / future-scope boundaries
02
Project classification — Level 0-4 × domain complexity Low/Medium/High → determines step depth
03
Target users + personas — extract from brief if available, 1-3 personas with JTBD format
04
Functional Requirements — numbered FR-01, FR-02..., MoSCoW priority, user story format, independently testable
05
Domain-specific section — only for High complexity (HIPAA/GDPR/SOC2/PCI compliance mapping)
06
Innovation focus — articulate novel/differentiated vs table stakes, Blue Ocean Canvas, moat statements
07
Non-functional Requirements — quantified metric + threshold + measurement method (ISO 25010 taxonomy)
08
Success metrics — North Star + supporting OKRs, baseline + target (12 weeks) + measurement source
09
Vision + Differentiators — only for Level 3-4, theory of change + positioning
10
Executive Summary — 1-page Problem/Solution/Impact/Ask, written last, placed first
11
Assumptions + Risks — explicit assumption mapping + risk register (probability × impact + mitigation)
12
Review — completeness + consistency + clarity check; BMAD tip: use different LLM for validation
13
Save + handoff — 3 files (prd.md + addendum.md + decision-log.md), invoke bmad-help

3 output files:

File Content
prd.mdMain document — all 13 sections (executive summary at top, then ordered sections)
addendum.mdLong-form details (data dictionary, API contract mocks, persona deep-dives) keep main PRD clean
decision-log.mdDate | Decision | Alternatives | Rationale | Owner — preserve "why we did X" 6 months later
why

PRD is contract between product and engineering. It defines what to build and for whom. Without a PRD, architecture builds the wrong thing, stories miss requirements, and QA does not know what to verify.

Scale-adaptive depth is a major v6 improvement. A single PRD template does not fit every project — over-documenting small work wastes time, while under-documenting complex work misses requirements. Classification matrix in step 02:

Level Scope PRD depth
Level 0Bug fix, quick change3 sections minimum (FR + DoD + acceptance) — does not need exec summary
Level 1Single feature5-7 sections — skip vision/risks unless explicit need
Level 2Epic (multiple features)8-10 sections — include success metrics + assumptions
Level 3Full product11-13 sections — full PRD, includes vision section
Level 4Platform / multi-productAll 13 sections + extended addendum (multi-tenancy, ecosystem)

Domain complexity adjustment: High complexity (regulated industries) forces step 05 to dig deep even at a low level. Healthcare app Level 1 still must full HIPAA section.

Numbered FRs is backbone of traceability downstream:

FR-03 in PRD
→
FileUploadService in arch
→
Story S2 references FR-03
→
QA verify FR-03 covered

Without numbering = "lost in implementation" — nobody knows which FRs are covered or missing. Architecture has FileUploadService but PRD does not have FR related = over-engineering. PRD has FR-08 but architecture does not have component address = cohesion gap, ship bug.

Quantified NFRs prevent disputes:

Vague NFR Quantified NFR
"High performance""p99 latency < 500ms with 1000 concurrent users, measured via load test"
"Secure""OAuth 2.0 auth, AES-256 at rest, TLS 1.3 in transit, SOC2 compliant"
"Reliable""99.9% uptime SLA, RPO 5 minutes, RTO 30 minutes"
"Scalable""Support 10K concurrent users initially, scale to 100K within 6 months"

"High performance" could mean 100ms or 1s — the difference drives completely different architecture (caching strategy, DB choice). Quantifying at the PRD level prevents post-ship battles between dev and stakeholder.

Explicit out-of-scope is as important as in-scope. PRD with only in-scope items = unlimited creep — stakeholders naturally expand expectations. Explicit out-of-scope list prevents drift, force prioritization conversations upfront.

Self-validation antipattern: PM self validate PRD = confirmation bias. Step 12 BMAD tip: use different LLM for validation. Same model that wrote PRD finds its own writing consistent — blind spots persist. Switch model surface inconsistencies same person missed.

Pre-mortem in step 11 by Daniel Kahneman improves forecast accuracy by 30%. Imagine PRD failed at Phase 4 — what went wrong? Top 5 reasons → risk register with mitigation. Front-loading identification reduces 80% of bad surprises during implementation.

Decision log immutable: records "why we chose X over Y" at the decision point. 6 months after team does not re-debate. Without log, lessons lost and mistakes repeated.

how

Activation: John (PM) own. Invoke through /bmad-agent-bmm-pm then command. Mode auto-detect based on existing PRD file:

User intent Mode dispatched Workflow path
No prd.md existsCreatesteps-c/ directory loaded
prd.md exists, user invoke VPValidatesteps-v/ directory loaded
prd.md exists, user invoke EPEditsteps-e/ directory loaded
Configuration Detail
AgentJohn (📋 Product Manager) — _bmad/bmm/agents/pm.md
Workflow root_bmad/bmm/workflows/2-planning-workflows/create-prd/
Create stepssteps-c/step-01.md ... step-13.md
Validate stepssteps-v/step-01.md ... step-06.md
Edit stepssteps-e/step-01.md ... step-04.md
Output paths_bmad-output/planning-artifacts/{prd,addendum,decision-log}.md

Classification matrix in step 02 — automatically determine which steps skip:

Level / Complexity Low Medium High
L0steps 01, 04, 12, 13+ step 11 (risks)+ step 05 (compliance)
L1steps 01-04, 07, 12, 13+ step 08 (metrics)+ step 05
L2steps 01-08, 11-13+ step 10 (exec sum)+ step 05
L3steps 01-04, 06-13+ step 09 (vision)+ step 05
L4All 13 stepsAll 13 stepsAll 13 steps + extended addendum

FR format in step 04 — Each FR has 5 components mandatory:

FR-03 · User uploads patient insurance documents
Description: Healthcare worker can upload PDF, JPG, or PNG files containing insurance documents. System extracts text via OCR, identifies key fields (policy number, coverage limits, expiration), stores encrypted.
Priority: Must (MoSCoW)
User story: As John (Operations Manager), I want to upload an insurance document, so that I can get coverage answers without manually typing details.
Notes: Implementation detail (which OCR vendor) defer to architecture — not in PRD.

FR independence test: each FR must independently testable. If FR-03 fails its test, FR-04 should still pass — do not chain dependencies inside FR descriptions. Chains live in the story dependency graph (Phase 3), not PRD.

Compliance mapping in step 05 for High complexity:

Regulation Domain triggers Architectural impact
HIPAAUS healthcare, PHI dataBAA agreements, audit log retention 6 years, access controls, encryption at rest + transit
GDPREU users, personal dataRight to deletion, data residency EU, consent management, breach notification 72h
SOC2SaaS enterprise sales5 trust principles, annual audit, access reviews, change management
PCI-DSSPayment card dataTokenization, segmented network, 4 levels based on volume
COPPAChildren under 13 (US)Parental consent, limited data collection, no behavioral advertising

Mary does not self assume HIPAA for every healthcare app — ask data type and jurisdiction before.

NFR taxonomy in step 07 by ISO 25010:

Quality attribute Example metric format
Performancep95 latency < 200ms, throughput > 1000 RPS at peak
Availability99.9% uptime SLA, RPO 5min, RTO 30min
SecurityOAuth 2.0, AES-256 at rest, TLS 1.3 in transit, CVE patches within 7 days
Scalability10K users initial, 100K within 6 months, horizontal scaling auto-triggered at 70% CPU
MaintainabilityOnboarding new dev < 2 weeks, test coverage > 80%, complexity < 10 per function
AccessibilityWCAG 2.1 AA compliance, contrast 4.5:1, keyboard nav 100%

Risk register format in step 11:

Field Content
Risk IDR-01, R-02... (globally unique)
CategoryMarket / Technical / Team / Regulatory / Financial
Description1-2 sentences specific specific (not "could fail")
ProbabilityH/M/L
ImpactH/M/L
Priority scoreP×I (HH = critical, HL/LH = moderate, LL = low)
MitigationAction + owner + trigger condition
StatusOpen / Mitigated / Accepted

Validation gate in step 12 — checklist:

A
Completeness — required sections present, every FR has ID + AC + priority, Every NFR has a metric + threshold
B
Consistency — FR-IDs referenced consistently, exec sum match body, NFRs not contradict FRs
C
Clarity — a third-party reader can understand it, no jargon-only sections, vague language flagged
D
Different LLM tip — switch model for validation step to avoid self-confirmation bias (BMAD official recommendation)

Handoff:

02·A done
→
02·B UX Design
→
03·A Architecture

Path 1: project has a UI component → UX first, then Architecture

02·A done
→
03·A Architecture

Path 2: backend-only/API project → Architecture direct

Press ESC or click outside to close
Workflow · 02 · B · Phase 02 Planning · Run by UX Expert (Sally)
UX Design
Comprehensive UX specification — user journeys + Information Architecture + low-fi wireframes + interaction patterns + component specs + responsive + accessibility WCAG 2.1 AA + design system. Optional but strongly recommended for UI projects.
what

UX Design workflow is optional but strongly recommended if the product has a UI component. Sally (UX) translates PRD requirements into interactive design specifications driving frontend architecture choices downstream.

10 steps comprehensive:

01
Load PRD + extract UX-relevant FRs/NFRs — identify FRs with UI surface, NFRs constrain UX (perf, a11y, responsive)
02
User journey mapping — 5 stages × persona × emotion curve, identify moments of truth at transitions
03
Information Architecture — sitemap + navigation structure (top/side/breadcrumb) + content taxonomy via card sorting
04
Low-fi wireframes — mobile-first sketches, gray boxes + placeholder text, focus layout/hierarchy not aesthetics
05
Interaction patterns — forms / search / modals / notifications / loading states standardized
06
Component specs — Atomic Design (atoms/molecules/organisms), states (default/hover/active/disabled/error/loading), variants
07
Responsive behavior — breakpoints (mobile <640, tablet 640-1024, desktop >1024), content priority per breakpoint
08
Accessibility — WCAG 2.1 AA: contrast 4.5:1, keyboard nav, screen reader, focus visible, error identification, motion preferences
09
Design system — W3C design tokens: color/typography/spacing/shadow/radius/motion, semantic naming
10
Finalize + handoff — save DESIGN.md + EXPERIENCE.md, invoke bmad-help → Architecture next

Output structure — two peer spines, DESIGN.md (visual identity + tokens) and EXPERIENCE.md (IA, behavior, states, interactions, a11y, journeys); EXPERIENCE.md references DESIGN.md tokens by name:

Section Content
User journeysPer persona, 5 stages with emotion + moments of truth
Information ArchitectureSitemap diagram + nav structure decision + content taxonomy
WireframesASCII/text description or Figma link, 5-10 key screens
Interaction patternsPattern library — trigger + behavior + edge cases for 5+ patterns
Component specsAtomic design — atoms list + molecules + organisms with states + variants
Responsive behaviorBreakpoint table + per-breakpoint layout decisions
AccessibilityWCAG 2.1 AA checklist + ARIA patterns specified
Design systemToken references — color scale + typography scale + spacing grid
why

UX informs Architecture — run UX before Architecture is an important BMAD v6 design choice. Frontend framework choice depends on components, a11y library depends on WCAG level, state management depends on interaction complexity.

UX decision Architecture implication
Complex interactive components (drag-drop, real-time collab)State management approach (Redux/Zustand/Jotai), WebSocket layer needed
Heavy form-based workflowForm library choice (React Hook Form/Formik), validation strategy (client + server)
Mobile-first priorityFramework choice (React Native vs PWA vs native), build pipeline
WCAG 2.1 AA requirementA11y-first component library (Radix/Headless UI/Reach), audit tooling
Design system formalizedToken compilation pipeline (Style Dictionary), CSS-in-JS choice

Without UX-spec, devs decide UI per epic themselves → inconsistency, rework. UX-spec sets boundaries for frontend implementation choices.

Static screens hide flow — journey reveals where pain lives. Research show most UX problems live at transitions between stages, not in the stages themselves:

Transition Common UX problem
Signup → first actionBiggest dropoff — overwhelm vs empowerment
Trial → paidPricing page friction, value not perceived yet
Error → recoveryError messages cryptic, no clear next step
Multi-step formLost progress on validation error

Mobile-first reveals priorities: limited space forces what is actually important to surface. 70% web traffic mobile — mobile-first is not an afterthought. Build desktop first, retrofit mobile = compromise mobile experience always.

Accessibility is legal + ethical, not nice-to-have:

Region Legal requirement
USADA (Americans with Disabilities Act) — lawsuits common for public-facing apps
EUEAA (European Accessibility Act) — mandatory by June 2025 for many digital products
UKEquality Act 2010 — public sector mandatory, private sector recommended
CanadaAODA (Accessibility for Ontarians with Disabilities Act)

Cost of retrofit accessibility 10x cost of build-in. Improvements also help all users — SEO, keyboard power users, mobile contexts (sun glare, single hand).

Design tokens vs literal values:

Literal naming (anti-pattern) Semantic naming (correct)
color.blue.500color.bg.primary
spacing.16pxspacing.scale.4
font.size.20pxfont.scale.lg

Brand refresh = 20 token updates, not 200 screen edits. Critical for Level 3-4 projects with multiple feature teams. 1 token change propagate everywhere — semantic naming describes role, not appearance.

Component without states = under-spec. Specifying Button = 1 visual only → dev guess hover/active/disabled/error → inconsistency across screens. Each component needs 5 states + a11y spec. Atomic Design (Brad Frost) enforces composition: atoms → molecules → organisms → templates → pages — reusable and maintainable.

Peak-end rule (Kahneman): users remember peak emotion + end emotion. Design deliberately 1 peak moment (e.g. instant aha) + 1 strong end moment (e.g. clean completion). Skip = miss psychological retention opportunity.

how

Activation: Sally invoke through /bmad-agent-bmm-ux-designer then CU.

Configuration Detail
AgentSally (🎨 UX Designer) — _bmad/bmm/agents/ux-designer.md
Commandbmad-ux · short-code CU
Workflow path_bmad/bmm/workflows/2-planning-workflows/ux-design/
Step location10 micro-files: step-01.md ... step-10.md
Output path_bmad-output/planning-artifacts/DESIGN.md + EXPERIENCE.md

Requirements traceability matrix in step 01:

FR ID FR title UX implication
FR-03User uploads insurance documentDrag-drop zone + file picker + progress indicator + error states (file too large, wrong type) + success confirmation
FR-05User searches insurance policiesSearch bar with autocomplete + filter sidebar + results list with pagination + empty state design
NFR-02p95 latency < 200msAvoid heavy animations > 200ms; skeleton screens for > 200ms loads; optimistic UI updates
NFR-04WCAG 2.1 AAFocus states required, contrast 4.5:1 enforced, ARIA labels mandatory, keyboard nav 100% coverage

User journey mapping protocol in step 02 — 5 stages × emotion curve:

Stage Touchpoints Target emotion Moment of truth
AwarenessSearch, referral, adCuriousFirst impression: does this solve my problem?
ConsiderationLanding page, pricing, reviewsCautiously optimisticPricing page — value perceived vs cost
OnboardingSignup, tutorial, first taskConfidentFirst 5 minutes — overwhelm or empowerment?
First useCore feature, aha momentDelightedValue realization — does it actually work?
HabitualDaily/weekly engagementReliant, advocateRetention loop — what brings them back?

Sally tag transitions with danger level: high = prioritize design attention. Onboarding → first use is often the biggest drop-off transition.

Card sorting in step 03 — discover user mental model:

Type Method When to use
OpenUser groups items + names categories themselvesGreenfield project, no existing IA assumptions
ClosedUser fits items into predefined categoriesValidate existing IA, identify miscategorized items
HybridSome categories predefined, others openRefining existing IA with novel content

Tree testing validate IA: can user find feature X within ≤3 clicks? If not → IA needs rework.

Crazy 8s technique in step 04 — sketch 8 variations / 8 minutes before commit:

Variation 1
→
V2
→
V3
→
...
→
V8

Generate volume quickly, pushing the mind beyond the first obvious design. Then pick best 1-2 for low-fi wireframe.

Interaction patterns library in step 05:

Pattern Trigger + behavior + edge cases
Form validationOn-blur vs on-submit decision, inline vs summary error display, field-level vs form-level errors, async validation strategy
SearchAutocomplete trigger (after 2 chars), debounce timing (300ms), filters interaction, pagination strategy (infinite scroll vs numbered)
ModalsWhen to use (vs inline), dismissible via ESC + backdrop click, focus trap mandatory, return focus on close
NotificationsToast (transient) vs inline (persistent) vs system (OS-level) vs email trigger conditions
Loading statesSkeleton screens for > 200ms, spinners for < 200ms, progress bar for known duration, optimistic UI updates

Atomic Design structure in step 06:

Level Examples Specification depth
AtomsButton, Input, Label, Icon, Badge5 states (default/hover/active/disabled/error) + variants (primary/secondary/destructive/ghost) + a11y
MoleculesFormField (Label + Input + ErrorMsg), SearchBar, Card, MenuItemComposition of atoms + interaction behavior
OrganismsHeader, DataTable, Modal, SidebarComposition of molecules + responsive behavior
TemplatesDashboardLayout, AuthLayoutPage-level layout, slot placement for organisms
PagesHomePage, SettingsPageTemplate instances with real content

Component state matrix per atom — required for handoff to Dev:

State Visual change Behavior
DefaultBase styleIdle, awaiting interaction
HoverSubtle bg change, cursor pointerMouse over (desktop only)
Active/PressedDarker bg, slight scaleMouse down or tap in progress
FocusOutline ring (a11y mandatory)Keyboard navigation lands
Disabled50% opacity, cursor not-allowedCannot interact, ARIA disabled
ErrorRed border + icon + messageValidation failed
LoadingSpinner replacing labelAsync action in progress

Responsive breakpoints in step 07:

Breakpoint Width Layout pattern
Mobile< 640pxSingle column, bottom nav, hamburger menu, full-width inputs
Tablet640-1024px2 columns possible, side nav optional, mixed layout
Desktop> 1024pxMulti-column, expanded nav, sidebar persistent, dense data tables

Per-breakpoint decisions: navigation pattern (bottom → side → top), content priority (mobile hides secondary actions), component reflow (card stack → grid).

WCAG 2.1 AA checklist in step 08:

Requirement Acceptance criteria
Contrast4.5:1 for normal text (16px+), 3:1 for large text (24px+ or 18px+ bold)
Keyboard navAll interactive elements reachable via Tab, focus order logical, no keyboard traps
Screen readerMeaningful labels (aria-label or visible), alt text for informational images, ARIA roles where needed
Focus visibleClear focus indicator on all interactive elements (don't outline: none unless replaced)
Error identificationErrors described in text (not only color), associated with input via aria-describedby
MotionRespects prefers-reduced-motion media query, no autoplay videos > 5s

Design tokens in step 09 — W3C standard format, semantic naming:

Token category Example semantic tokens
Colorcolor.bg.primary / color.text.body / color.border.subtle / color.feedback.error
Typographyfont.size.xs/sm/md/lg/xl / font.weight.normal/medium/bold / font.lineHeight.tight/normal/loose
Spacing4px base grid: spacing.scale.1 (4px) / scale.2 (8px) / scale.4 (16px) / scale.8 (32px)
Shadowshadow.sm/md/lg/xl — semantic levels of elevation
Radiusradius.none/sm/md/lg/full
Motionduration.fast/normal/slow / easing.standard/decelerate/accelerate

Reference systems: Material Design 3, Apple HIG, IBM Carbon, Shopify Polaris, Tailwind. Pick closest to team's tech stack rather than mix random.

Handoff:

02·B done
→
03·A Architecture

UX-spec drives frontend framework + component library + a11y tooling choices

Press ESC or click outside to close
Workflow · 03 · A · Phase 03 Solutioning · Run by Architect (Winston)
Architecture
REQUIRED in Phase 3 — intent-routed bmad-architecture skill (create / update / validate), lean-spine model with breadth-coverage rubric and Reviewer Gate. Produces ARCHITECTURE-SPINE.md as source of truth (+ ADRs, optional renderings). Supersedes the deprecated fixed 11-step architecture flow (retained below for reference): C4 diagrams + tech stack + data model + API design + integration + security STRIDE + deployment + NFR validation ATAM + risks FMEA.
what

Architecture workflow is required gate before Phase 4. Winston (Architect) produce comprehensive technical specification that every dev agent downstream reference. ADRs (Architecture Decision Records) record why, not just what — 6 months after team does not re-debate.

8 steps, intent-routed (create / update / validate):

01
Detect intent & activation mode — Create / Update / Validate, headless vs interactive, honors forwarded fields from the deprecated bmad-create-architecture shim
02
Choose Coaching vs Fast path — Coaching (default, open-ended elicitation) or Fast (draft with [ASSUMPTION] tags); also asks whether the spine is the only deliverable
03
Read the input to know the job — spec package, raw idea, existing codebase, feature slice, or an existing spine to extend; inherit a parent spine's invariants read-only
04
Establish paradigm & seed — lead with a named design paradigm, recommend a verified current starter for greenfield, ratify existing conventions for brownfield
05
Log decisions to the memlog — every decision/constraint/version/assumption/open-question as one append-only line; surviving decisions become stable AD-n entries
06
Distill the spine — write ARCHITECTURE-SPINE.md from the memlog against a breadth-coverage rubric; no dimension left silently undecided
07
Reviewer Gate — deterministic lint_spine.py + rubric walker + parallel reviewer-lens subagents; Validate intent renders a bespoke HTML report
08
Renderings, close & handoff — optional purpose-scoped artifact, status: final, recommend bmad-spec then bmad-create-epics-and-stories / bmad-create-story

Supersedes the deprecated fixed 11-step flow (Init & Load PRD → C4 diagrams → tech stack ADRs → data model → API design → integration patterns → security STRIDE → deployment 12-factor → NFR validation ATAM → risk FMEA → Finalize+ADRs), which produced architecture.md + ADRs/*.md as its primary output rather than a spine.

3 output artifacts:

File Purpose
ARCHITECTURE-SPINE.mdPrimary source of truth — C4 diagrams + tech stack + data model + API + integration + security + deployment
ADRs/ADR-001-database.md
ADRs/ADR-002-api-style.md
ADRs/...		1 file per major decision, numbered immutable. Format Michael Nygard: Title/Status/Context/Decision/Consequences/Alternatives
project-context.md"Constitution" for AI dev agents — patterns, conventions, do/don't anti-patterns. Propagate downstream to every dev session
why

Architecture is required in Phase 3. Without architecture, multi-epic projects suffer syndrome "REST in epic 1, GraphQL in epic 2" = integration nightmare. Architecture = consistent decisions across all agents + sprints.

Catching misalignment in Phase 3 costs 10x less than in Phase 4. ADRs (Michael Nygard pattern) record why, not just what — eliminates future re-debate.

v6 ordering insight: epics & stories are created AFTER architecture (reversing pre-v6 order). Stories now reflect technical decisions — knows patterns, auth approach, DB. Pre-architecture stories miss tech context → rework mid-sprint.

Architecture drivers - top NFRs drive architecture more than FRs:

NFR driver Architecture choice forced
p95 latency < 200msRead replica, CDN, in-memory cache (Redis), edge computing — Architecture must include a caching layer
99.99% uptimeMulti-region active-active, automatic failover, chaos engineering — fundamentally different from a 99% topology
10K → 100K users in 6 monthsHorizontal scaling foundation from day 1, not vertical-scale-then-rewrite
HIPAA complianceBAA agreements, audit log architecture, encryption everywhere, access control granularity
GDPR right-to-deletionData architecture must support hard delete + cascade, not soft delete only

Without explicit drivers, team debate style preference instead of tradeoffs.

C4 model layered abstraction:

Level Audience Stability
ContextEvery stakeholder (including non-technical)Stable — system + external actors few change
ContainerEngineers must understand major building blocksMost useful — stable through refactors, code changes but containers few change
ComponentSenior devs on specific containerLess stable — refactor changes component boundaries
CodeDevs working on specific classLeast stable, optional — only for critical paths

Sweet spot is Container diagram — universal language between engineers, few churn through refactors.

ADR-driven decisions prevent re-debate:

Without ADR (anti-pattern) With ADR
"We chose PostgreSQL because team knows it" (verbal, undocumented)ADR-001 with Context (data has relational structure), Decision (PostgreSQL 15), Consequences (operational maturity + vertical scale ceiling at 100GB), Alternatives (MongoDB rejected — no transactions for financial data; DynamoDB rejected — query flexibility limits)
6 months later: "Why didn't we use MongoDB?" — re-debate6 months later: read ADR-001 — answer is there, no re-debate

Boring technology principle: innovation budget limited. Spend on differentiator, not on commodity. PostgreSQL > novel NoSQL except when specifically needed. Boring = mature + well-documented + widely adopted = predictable operations.

Security retrofit costs 10-100x more than building it in. STRIDE per component surfaces implicit attack surfaces. Compliance (SOC2, ISO 27001) requires documented security architecture — does not have = compliance audit fails.

Observability triad mandatory before launch:

Pillar Question answered Tools
LogsWhat happened?Structured JSON, centralized (Datadog/Splunk/Loki), retention policy
MetricsIs system healthy?System (CPU/RAM/latency) + business (DAU/revenue) (Prometheus, CloudWatch)
TracesWhy slow?OpenTelemetry standard, distributed tracing for request flows (Jaeger/Tempo)

Retrofitting observability painful. Need enough before shipping — debug nightmare without.

NFR validation closed-loop in step 09. Architecture must prove each NFR covered, not assume. Without explicit mapping, NFRs become an afterthought is discovered when fail.

how

Activation: Winston invoke through /bmad-agent-bmm-architect then CA.

Configuration Detail
AgentWinston (🏗️ System Architect) — _bmad/bmm/agents/architect.md
Commandbmad-architecture · short-code CA
Workflow path_bmad/bmm/workflows/3-solutioning-workflows/architecture/
Step locationIntent-routed skill (create/update/validate), not fixed step files — see references/reviewer-gate.md and references/headless.md
Output pathsARCHITECTURE-SPINE.md (source of truth) + ADRs/*.md + project-context.md

ADR template Michael Nygard format:

# ADR-005: API style — REST with OpenAPI 3.0

Status: Accepted (2026-03-15)

Context: Need to expose backend functionality to web frontend (React) and mobile clients (React Native). NFR-02 requires p95 < 200ms. Team has REST experience but not GraphQL.

Decision: Use REST API following OpenAPI 3.0 spec. URL versioning (/v1/). JWT auth via Authorization header.

Consequences:
+ Lower learning curve, team productive immediately
+ Mature tooling (Swagger UI, code generators)
+ Easier debugging (browser DevTools native)
− N+1 query risk for mobile (will need GraphQL-like field selection or BFF pattern later)
− Versioning ceremony for breaking changes

Alternatives considered:
- GraphQL: rejected — learning curve, complexity for simple CRUD doesn't justify benefits at current scale
- gRPC: rejected — not browser-native, requires gateway, overkill for current needs
- Event-driven (AsyncAPI): rejected — not appropriate for primary read-heavy use case

Tech stack ADR coverage in step 03 — typical projects have these ADRs minimum:

ADR ID Decision area Example for healthcare SaaS
ADR-001Primary databasePostgreSQL 15 — relational data, transactions, mature ops
ADR-002Backend language/frameworkNode.js + TypeScript + Express — team expertise, ecosystem
ADR-003Frontend frameworkReact 18 + Next.js — SSR for SEO, team expertise
ADR-004Caching strategyRedis cluster — sub-ms reads, distributed
ADR-005API styleREST + OpenAPI 3.0 (see template above)
ADR-006Auth approachOAuth 2.0 + Auth0 — managed identity, MFA, audit
ADR-007Cloud providerAWS — HIPAA-eligible services, BAA available
ADR-008Container orchestrationECS Fargate — managed, no K8s ops burden at our scale
ADR-009Observability stackDatadog — APM + logs + metrics + tracing in one
ADR-010Message queueSQS + SNS — managed, scales infinitely, fits AWS choice

API style decision matrix in step 05:

Style Best for Contract artifact
RESTCRUD-heavy, resource-oriented, public APIsOpenAPI 3.0 spec
GraphQLFlexible queries, complex data graphs, mobile (avoid over-fetching)SDL (Schema Definition Language)
gRPCInternal microservices, streaming, performance-critical.proto files (Protocol Buffers)
Event-drivenAsync workflows, decoupling, scale via queuesAsyncAPI spec

Resilience patterns in step 06:

A
Circuit Breaker — fail fast when external service down; prevent cascade failures. State machine: closed → open → half-open
B
Retry with backoff + jitter — exponential backoff for transient failures, jitter avoid thundering herd
C
Dead Letter Queue — catch failed messages instead of silent drop, manual review queue
D
Timeout strategy — explicit timeouts each external call, prevent hanging requests
E
Bulkhead — isolate resources per dependency, one slow service not block others

STRIDE threat model in step 07 — per component:

Threat Question Mitigation example
SpoofingCan someone impersonate identity?Strong auth (OAuth + MFA), service-to-service mTLS
TamperingCan data be modified in transit/rest?TLS 1.3, HMAC signatures, checksums, immutable audit log
RepudiationCan user deny actions?Audit log with cryptographic signatures, non-repudiable timestamps
Info disclosureCan data leak unintentionally?Encryption at rest, RBAC, PII tokenization, secret scanning
DoSCan service be made unavailable?Rate limiting, WAF, auto-scaling, CDN, DDoS protection (Cloudflare/Shield)
Elevation of privilegeCan user gain unauthorized access?Principle of least privilege, role separation, regular access reviews

Deployment architecture in step 08 — observability triad + 12-factor:

12-factor principle Implementation
Config in envNo code, secrets in AWS Secrets Manager / Vault
Stateless processesSession in Redis, not in-memory; allows horizontal scaling
DisposabilityFast startup (< 30s), graceful shutdown SIGTERM handling
Dev-prod parityDocker compose mirrors prod stack, not snowflake setups
Logs as event streamsstdout/stderr only, not write files; aggregator handles

NFR validation table in step 09 (ATAM-light):

NFR Solution components Quantified expectation Validation method
NFR-01Read replica + CDN + Redis cachep95 ~150ms expectedLoad test 1000 concurrent users, k6 scripts
NFR-02Multi-AZ deployment + auto-scaling + health checks99.9% uptimeChaos engineering: kill AZ, measure recovery
NFR-03OAuth 2.0 + MFA + AES-256 + audit logSOC2 compliantAnnual audit by external assessor

FMEA scoring in step 10 — top risks:

Risk S O D RPN (S×O×D)
Redis cluster fails, cache miss storm hits DB84396 — High
Auth0 vendor outage blocks all logins92236 — Medium
Database migration causes downtime > SLA73484 — High
Cost overrun > 2x estimate at scale555125 — Critical

Handoff:

03·A done
→
03·B Epics & Stories
→
03·C Readiness Check

PM (John) returns to create stories with tech context from architecture; PM (John) validate cohesion

Press ESC or click outside to close
Workflow · 03 · B · Phase 03 Solutioning · Run by PM (John) + Architect (Winston)
Epics & Stories
Decomposition of PRD requirements into epics (user capabilities) and stories (INVEST units) — created AFTER architecture per v6 improvement. Stories reflect technical decisions, AC by BDD/Gherkin format.
what

Epics & Stories workflow splits the PRD into actionable work units for Phase 4. PM (John) lead, Architect (Winston) collab for technical context. v6 improvement: run AFTER architecture, not before.

7 steps:

01
Load PRD + architecture — build FR ↔ component traceability matrix, flag gaps (empty rows = uncovered FR; empty cols = over-engineered component)
02
Group FRs into epics — user-deliverable capabilities (NOT tech layers), validate INVEST-at-epic-level (independent / valuable / 2-6 weeks)
03
Epic ordering (WSJF) — (User-Business Value + Time Criticality + Risk Reduction) / Job Size = priority; first epic must be independent
04
Story breakdown (INVEST) — split each epic into stories Independent/Negotiable/Valuable/Estimable/Small/Testable; SPIDR patterns if too large
05
AC writing (BDD/Gherkin) — Given/When/Then format, 3-7 AC per story, cover happy + edge + error paths
06
Dependency mapping — graph nodes (stories), edges (blockers); identify critical path; flag >3 deps = signal split
07
Save + handoff — epic-{N}.md per epic with stories nested, invoke bmad-help → Readiness Check

Epic file structure:

Section Content
FrontmatterepicId, status, owner, sequence, wsjfScore
Epic descriptionUser capability articulated, link to FRs covered
Acceptance criteria (epic-level)What it means epic = done
Stories (nested)S1, S2, S3... numbered, per story has full AC + tech context + DoD
DependenciesExternal epic dependencies + cross-epic story dependencies
RisksEpic-specific risks (vs project-level in PRD)

Story sections inside epic:

Section Content
Story metadataID (e.g., epic-01-S1), title, owner, estimate (story points or days)
User storyAs [persona] / I want to [action] / So that [outcome]
Acceptance criteria3-7 ACs in Gherkin format (Given/When/Then)
Technical contextFiles to touch, patterns to follow, API contracts reference (from architecture)
DependenciesHard blockers / Soft dependencies / Independent
Definition of DoneInherited from project-context.md or explicit override
why

most important v6 improvement: epics & stories are created AFTER architecture, not before. Reason:

Pre-v6 ordering (Stories before Architecture) v6 ordering (Architecture before Stories)
Stories created based on PRD aloneStories reflect architecture decisions
Tech context discovered mid-implementationTech context known upfront — file paths, patterns, API contracts
Stories often need rework when architecture surface tradeoffsStories solid, less rework
Dev agent must context-load PRD + architecture for every storyStory self-contained — references tech context already
Estimate accuracy poor (no tech context)Estimate accurate — knows complexity from architecture

Quote from BMAD docs: "Epics and stories are now created after architecture. This produces better quality stories."

Tech-layer epics anti-pattern — epics must is user capabilities, not backend/frontend split:

Bad epic naming (tech layer) Good epic naming (user capability)
"Backend", "Frontend", "Database""User onboarding", "Payment processing"
"API layer", "Auth service""Document upload & search", "Admin dashboard"
Cannot demo standaloneDemo-able standalone — stakeholders see progress
Integration nightmare end-of-projectIntegration tested epic-by-epic, less risk

Acid test: "Can I demo this epic to a non-technical stakeholder and they see value?" If no → tech-layer epic, redo.

WSJF (Weighted Shortest Job First) prioritization by SAFe framework — ensure ensure first sprints deliver demoable value:

WSJF = (User-Business Value + Time Criticality + Risk Reduction) / Job Size

Each component scored 1-10 relatively (not absolute):
• Value: Direct revenue/UX impact
• Criticality: Cost of delaying this epic
• Risk reduction: How much does shipping this de-risk project?
• Size: Estimated weeks of work

Risky epics early = fail fast while resources remain. Pivot while still has runway. Value-first = demonstrate value early = stakeholder confidence + feedback loop.

INVEST sweet spot for stories (1-3 days dev work):

Story too large (> 5 days) Story too small (< 0.5 day)
Uncertainty → estimate wrong → sprint slipsOverhead (PR review, deploy) > value
Risk too concentrated in one work unitStory fragmentation, lose coherence
Hard to estimate accuratelySprint planning churn
Hard to demo independentlyNo meaningful deliverable

SPIDR splitting patterns when the story is too large:

Pattern Method Example
SpikeSeparate exploration/researchStory A1 = research OCR vendors; Story A2 = integrate chosen vendor
PathSplit by user journey pathsStory A1 = upload via web; Story A2 = upload via mobile
InterfaceSplit by API vs UI vs integrationStory A1 = backend API; Story A2 = frontend integration
DataSplit by data variationsStory A1 = PDF support; Story A2 = JPG/PNG support
RulesSplit by business rulesStory A1 = basic validation; Story A2 = complex rules

Most used = Interface split (API vs UI separate stories) — different skill sets, parallelize-able.

Gherkin/BDD format for AC — executable specification:

Vague AC (anti-pattern) Gherkin AC (correct)
"User can upload files""Given user authenticated, When they upload a PDF ≤ 10MB, Then file accepted, OCR triggered, progress shown"
"Works correctly""Given upload in progress, When user closes tab, Then upload resumes on return"
"Has good error handling""Given user uploads 10.1MB file, When upload starts, Then error shown: 'File exceeds 10MB limit'"

Dev writes tests directly from Gherkin = no interpretation needed. Vague AC = battle between dev + QA about what "done" means.

Dependency cycle detection mandatory. Story A → B → A means sprint never completes. PO checks in Readiness gate (03·C) by DFS cycle detection.

how

Activation: PM (John) primary. Invoke through /bmad-agent-bmm-pm then CE. Winston (Architect) collab — contribute tech context section per story.

Configuration Detail
Primary agentJohn (📋 PM)
Collab agentWinston (🏗️ Architect) — tech context contribution
Commandbmad-create-epics-and-stories · short-code CE
Workflow path_bmad/bmm/workflows/3-solutioning-workflows/epics-stories/
Step location7 micro-files: step-01.md ... step-07.md
Output path_bmad-output/epics/epic-{N}.md per epic

Traceability matrix in step 01:

FileUploadService AuthService SearchEngine NotificationService
FR-01 Login—X——
FR-02 Upload docXX—X
FR-03 Search—XX—
FR-04 Get notified—X—X

Empty row (FR with no X) = uncovered → architecture incomplete. Empty col (component with no X) = over-engineered → justify or remove.

Epic grouping strategy in step 02:

Capability mapping FRs grouped
Epic 1: User onboardingFR-01 Login, FR-08 Signup, FR-09 Profile setup, FR-10 First-run tutorial
Epic 2: Document managementFR-02 Upload, FR-03 Search, FR-05 Categorize, FR-11 Share
Epic 3: Insurance answers (core value)FR-04 AI Q&A, FR-12 Citation display, FR-13 Confidence indicator
Epic 4: Admin & opsFR-06 User mgmt, FR-07 Billing, FR-14 Audit log

Each FR assign into exactly 1 epic. Epic 1 too large (> 6 weeks) → split. Epic 4 too small (< 1 week) → merge with Epic 2 or accept as small.

WSJF scoring in step 03 — example:

Epic Value Criticality Risk Reduction Size WSJF Score Rank
1: Onboarding97353.82
2: Doc mgmt75482.03
3: Insurance answers109893.01 (sequenced first if risky)
4: Admin43233.04

Tiebreaker risk-first: Epic 3 (insurance answers) high risk reduction → sequence first even when WSJF ties with Epic 4. Validate hypothesis early.

INVEST checklist per story in step 04:

I
Independent — not hard block adjacent story when possible; soft deps OK
N
Negotiable — scope adjustable, not contract-frozen
V
Valuable — clear user/business benefit, traceable to FR
E
Estimable — dev can estimate without major uncertainty
S
Small — 1-3 days of dev work is ideal; > 5 days = split
T
Testable — ≥3 AC defined, verifiable outcomes

Gherkin AC writing in step 05 — story example:

Story epic-02-S3: User uploads insurance document

AC-1 (happy path):
Given user authenticated and on upload page
When user selects a PDF file ≤ 10MB and clicks Upload
Then file accepted, OCR triggered, progress shown 0-100%

AC-2 (file size limit):
Given user authenticated
When user attempts to upload PDF > 10MB
Then error shown "File exceeds 10MB limit", upload not started

AC-3 (wrong file type):
Given user authenticated
When user attempts to upload .exe or .zip
Then error shown "Only PDF, JPG, PNG accepted", upload not started

AC-4 (concurrent uploads):
Given upload in progress
When user uploads another file
Then second upload queued, both progress shown

Tech context in story (Winston contributes):

Files to touch:
• src/services/upload-service.ts — extend existing service
• src/components/UploadZone.tsx — new component
• src/api/upload.ts — new endpoint POST /v1/uploads

Patterns to follow:
• Repository pattern (see src/services/auth-service.ts)
• Use existing AuthService.getCurrentUser() — not re-implement
• Error handling via AppError class (see src/errors/)

API contracts:
• OpenAPI spec section /v1/uploads in docs/api.yaml
• Auth: JWT Bearer token required

Storage: S3 bucket insurance-docs-prod, KMS encrypted (ADR-007)

Dependency graph in step 06:

Type Definition Handling
Hard blockerB cannot start until A is doneSequential — B placed AFTER A in sprint sequence
Soft dependencyB easier if A done, but B can startPrefer ordering but parallelize OK
IndependentNo dependencySafe to parallelize in the same sprint

Critical path analysis (longest blocker chain) = minimum project duration. Flag stories with > 3 dependencies = signal split or re-sequence.

Handoff:

03·B done
→
03·C Readiness Check
→
04·A Sprint Planning

PM (John) validate cohesion + INVEST quality → Dev (Amelia) start sprint planning

Press ESC or click outside to close
Workflow · 03 · C · Phase 03 Solutioning · Run by PM (John) + Architect (Winston)
Implementation Readiness Check
REQUIRED quality gate before Phase 4 — PM (John) independent reviewer audit all planning artifacts for completeness/consistency/cohesion. Binary verdict PASS / CONCERNS / FAIL. BMAD tip: use different LLM for validation.
what

Readiness Check is required gate before project transition into Phase 4 Implementation. John (PM) — independent reviewer, separate from the person who created the PRD/architecture/stories — audit all artifacts for completeness, consistency, cohesion.

6 steps:

01
Load all planning artifacts — PRD + addendum + decision-log + architecture + ADRs + epics + UX spines (DESIGN.md + EXPERIENCE.md); build artifact inventory with timestamps
02
PRD review — completeness (required sections, FR/NFR/metric/threshold) + consistency (FR-IDs, exec sum match body) + FR quality
03
Architecture review — ADR quality (every major decision documented?) + NFR coverage (each NFR has component/pattern?) + risk register present?
04
Cohesion check — PRD ↔ Architecture traceability matrix; empty rows = uncovered FR; empty cols = over-engineered component
05
Epics INVEST validation — per-story scorecard, AC quality check (0-1 AC = insufficient; >7 = too large), dependency cycle check (DFS)
06
Binary verdict + report — PASS / CONCERNS / FAIL; save readiness-report.md; invoke bmad-help

Verdict criteria:

Verdict Criteria Action
PASS0 Blockers + ≤3 ConcernsProceed to Phase 4. Concerns documented in correction-log proactively
NEEDS REVIEW0 Blockers + >3 ConcernsProceed with documented caveats. Risks accepted explicitly. Team aware of trade-offs.
FAIL≥1 BlockerMust rework before Phase 4. PM/Architect re-engaged to fix blockers.

Output structure of readiness-report.md:

Section Content
VerdictPASS / CONCERNS / FAIL with explicit reasoning
Artifact inventoryFiles reviewed, timestamps, version checks
Per-section scorecardPRD / Architecture / Epics / UX — PASS/CONCERNS/FAIL per section
BlockersMust-fix list with owner + criteria for resolution
ConcernsShould-fix list with rationale (why concern, not blocker)
Traceability matrixPRD FRs × Architecture components — gaps highlighted
why

Readiness Check is last chance to catch planning bugs before they become implementation bugs (cost 10-100x to fix).

Bug discovered at phase Cost multiplier Example
Phase 1 (Analysis)1x baselineWrong persona → re-brainstorm: 1 hour
Phase 2 (Planning)3-5xMissing FR → update PRD: 1 day
Phase 3 (Solutioning)5-10xArchitecture mismatch → ADRs + rework: 3-7 days
Phase 4 (Implementation)10-100xCode rework + tests + deploy: 2-4 weeks

Readiness gate is cheapest possible bug discovery — pure review effort, no code yet.

PO ≠ PM by design: independent reviewer separate from the person who created the artifacts. Reduces self-confirmation bias. PM self validate PRD = miss own blind spots — same writer finds own writing consistent.

BMAD official tip: "Use a different LLM for validation step to avoid model self-validation". Switching model surface inconsistencies same model missed. Especially impactful for AI-driven development.

Cohesion gap = silent killer:

Gap type Symptom in Phase 4
PRD has FR-07, architecture has no addressing componentStory for FR-07 dispatched to Dev, but no architectural foundation → "where do I build this?"
Architecture has FileUploadService, no PRD FR references itOver-engineered component, no clear requirement, maintenance burden
PRD says HIPAA compliant, architecture has no audit log componentCompliance audit fails post-launch, scramble to retrofit
UX-spec specifies focus management, architecture not flag a11y libraryDev picks wrong library, retrofit accessibility 10x cost

Binary gates eliminate ambiguity. "Looks good but..." leaves project in limbo — sprint blocked indefinitely. Explicit verdict (PASS / CONCERNS / FAIL) unblocks decisions.

Severity level Definition Required action
BlockerWill fail in Phase 4 if not fixed (concrete failure mode)Must fix before proceed
Major ConcernWill cause significant problems but manageable workarounds existDocument, monitor, mitigate
Minor ConcernSuboptimal but won't block — improvement opportunityBacklog for future

Skeptical scrutiny default: John's PM-as-validator role is question-everything-once. False positives cheaper than missed bugs. Better to flag → human triage → dismiss false positive, than miss real Blocker.

Blameless framing per Etsy/Google blameless postmortem doctrine — focus on system/process gaps, not person blame. PRD has gap = process didn't catch it earlier, not "PM made mistake".

how

Activation: John (PM) invoke through /bmad-agent-bmm-po then IR. BMAD tip: use different LLM for session this if possible.

Configuration Detail
AgentJohn (📑 Product Owner) — _bmad/bmm/agents/po.md
Commandbmad-check-implementation-readiness · short-code IR
Workflow path_bmad/bmm/workflows/3-solutioning-workflows/readiness-check/
Step location6 micro-files: step-01.md ... step-06.md
Output path_bmad-output/planning-artifacts/readiness-report.md

Artifact inventory in step 01:

Required artifact Present? Action if missing
prd.mdRequiredCannot proceed — automatic FAIL
addendum.mdOptionalOK if PRD self-contained
decision-log.mdRequiredFAIL — decisions not traceable
ARCHITECTURE-SPINE.mdRequiredAutomatic FAIL
ADRs/*.mdRequired (≥3 ADRs)FAIL if <3 — major decisions not documented
project-context.mdRequiredFAIL — dev agents need conventions
epic-*.mdRequired (≥1)Automatic FAIL
DESIGN.md + EXPERIENCE.mdConditional (UI projects)FAIL if it has UI but no UX spines

Timestamp comparison: architecture newer than PRD = check for drift; epics older than architecture = stale, need refresh.

PRD review checklist in step 02:

Check PASS criteria Common failures
Required sections presentAll sections per Level classification non-emptyExec summary missing, success metrics blank
FR completenessEach FR has ID + title + description + priority + user storyFR without priority (MoSCoW), user story missing
NFR quantificationEach NFR has metric + threshold + measurement method"High performance" without numbers
FR-ID consistencyFR-03 referenced same way throughout"FR-3" in one place, "FR-03" in another
Exec sum match bodyExec summary points all detailed in bodyExec sum mentions feature not in FRs
NFR-FR consistencyNFRs not contradict FRs (vd: real-time vs batch processing)"Real-time" NFR but FR describes batch flow

Architecture review checklist in step 03:

Check PASS criteria
ADR coverageMajor decisions documented: database, language/framework, API style, auth, cloud, observability minimum
ADR qualityEach ADR has Context + Decision + Consequences + Alternatives sections non-empty
ADR status fieldEach ADR has status (Proposed/Accepted/Superseded), not blank
NFR addressedEach NFR from PRD has component/pattern in architecture address it
C4 diagrams presentContext + Container minimum, annotated with protocol/auth/sync
Risk register presentTop 5+ risks identified with FMEA scores + mitigation + owner
Compliance addressedFor regulated domains (HIPAA/GDPR/etc.), explicit components + ADRs

Cohesion check matrix in step 04 — core of readiness gate:

FileUploadService AuthService OCRService SearchEngine AuditLogService
